  1. Methods & Concepts
  2. What If, Ch. 1 — A definition of causal effect

What If, Ch. 1 — A definition of causal effect

Hernán & Robins, Causal Inference: What If — counterfactuals, individual vs. average effects, association ≠ causation

causal-inference
what-if
methods
Published

April 30, 2026

Note

Source: Hernán MA, Robins JM. Causal Inference: What If. Boca Raton: Chapman & Hall/CRC, 2020. Free PDF.

The summary below is in my own words — what I took away, why it matters to me as a biostatistician moving toward HEOR/RWE. For the actual material, read the book.

Concept

A causal effect, defined cleanly, is a contrast between two potential outcomes for the same unit under different treatments. For an individual, the causal effect of treatment \(A\) on outcome \(Y\) is \(Y^{a=1} - Y^{a=0}\) — the difference between what would happen if treated and what would happen if untreated. The fundamental problem of causal inference is that we only ever observe one of these two potential outcomes for each individual. The other is counterfactual.

Because individual causal effects are unidentifiable, causal inference operates at the average level: \(E[Y^{a=1}] - E[Y^{a=0}]\). We swap the impossible (knowing what would have happened to person \(i\)) for the merely difficult (estimating averages over groups under untestable assumptions).

Why it matters

This chapter is the conceptual fork in the road. Once you accept that:

  1. Causation is defined in terms of counterfactuals, not just associations,
  2. We can never observe both potential outcomes for the same person,
  3. So everything we do in causal inference is built on assumptions that license us to use observed data to estimate averages of unobserved counterfactuals —

…then everything else in the book (and in HEOR/RWE practice) makes sense as machinery for making that swap defensible. Propensity scores, IPTW, target trial emulation, IV — they’re all answers to the question: given that I can’t observe counterfactuals, under what assumptions can I still get \(E[Y^{a=1}] - E[Y^{a=0}]\) from data I do have?

For my work specifically: this is the lens I need to bring to RWE. A regression with covariates is not a causal analysis just because it controls for things. It becomes one when I can write down the counterfactual contrast it estimates and the assumptions under which my estimator equals that contrast.

My summary — key concepts that helped me understand

  • Potential outcomes notation \(Y^a\). \(Y^{a=1}\) is the outcome a person would have under treatment; \(Y^{a=0}\) is the outcome the same person would have under no treatment. These are properties of the person, not of the data. Most of them are unobserved by definition.

  • Individual causal effect = \(Y^{a=1} - Y^{a=0}\). Defined for a person. Almost never identifiable. The point of writing it down isn’t to estimate it — it’s to be precise about what you’d want to estimate in a perfect world.

  • Average causal effect = \(E[Y^{a=1}] - E[Y^{a=0}]\). This is the population-level quantity we can estimate, given assumptions. It’s not just “the average of individual effects” in any practically useful sense — it’s the contrast of two whole-population means under counterfactual treatment regimes.

  • Causation ≠ Association. \(E[Y \mid A=1] - E[Y \mid A=0]\) (an associational contrast) is not the same as \(E[Y^{a=1}] - E[Y^{a=0}]\) (a causal contrast). They’re equal only under conditions like exchangeability, which randomization buys you and observational data does not.

  • Three measures of effect show up: risk difference, risk ratio, odds ratio. Different scales, different uses. For HEOR work where I’ll be reporting to decision-makers, the risk difference / absolute scale is usually the most communicative; multiplicative measures collapse base-rate information.

  • Sharp vs. average null hypotheses. “Treatment had no effect on anyone” (sharp null) is much stronger than “average effect is zero.” A non-zero average can hide huge heterogeneity, and a zero average can hide opposite-direction effects in subgroups. This matters for HTE work.

Things to remember

  • Whenever I hear “the effect of \(A\) on \(Y\),” I should mentally translate to: “the contrast \(E[Y^{a=1}] - E[Y^{a=0}]\), under what assumptions, in what population.” If I can’t fill those blanks in, the claim isn’t well-defined yet.
  • A causal claim from observational data is a claim about counterfactuals plus a defense of the assumptions that license bridging from observed to counterfactual. The defense is the analysis. The model is just the bookkeeping.
  • “Confounding” in this framework is precisely the failure of exchangeability: \(Y^a \not\!\perp\!\!\!\perp A\). That’s a much sharper definition than the usual “a third variable that distorts the relationship.”

Code

No code in this chapter — it’s foundational. Code starts showing up around Chapter 11 (IP weighting) and 12 (g-formula). I’ll write up those entries as I get there, with R implementations using survey, WeightIt, and marginaleffects.

Status

Read. Re-read introduction and 1.4 (effect measures). Moving to Chapter 2 (randomized experiments) next.
